text.text:
If you go to The Happy Crane for one thing, make it the Peking duck — it's the star of the meal, hands down. The lacquered skin arrives shattering-crisp and mahogany-dark, sliced into neat squares that crackle the second you bite in, with just enough rendered fat clinging underneath to keep it rich without being greasy. The meat itself is tender and well-seasoned on its own, so even the slices without much skin still hold their own.

What really elevates it is the spread that comes with it: paper-thin house-made wrappers, a rich hoisin-style sauce, and a beautiful spread of julienned scallion and cucumber alongside jewel-toned pickled plum wedges for a tart, palate-cleansing contrast. Building your own wraps at the table turns it into an interactive, genuinely fun course rather than just a plate of meat.

I'd actually met Chef James previously at a collaboration dinner at Nisei with Chef David Yoshimura, so I was curious to see his cooking in his own space — and this meal did not disappoint. His hand is precise and confident across the board.
We rounded out the meal with several other dishes, all well executed:

Beef shin & celery root — deeply savory, glazed and fragrant with herbs, comfort food elevated

Firecracker shrimp — whole prawns with a crackly, herb-flecked shell, a fun and messy start to the meal

Maine sea scallop dumpling — delicate steamed wrappers crowned with glistening ikura, elegant and briny

Crispy maitake cheung fun — silky rice noodle rolls with crisp mushroom and chili crunch, a great textural contrast

Chasiu iberico pork jowl — awesome. Tender, fatty, char-kissed pork under crisp apple and fennel, one of the standout bites of the night

Every dish showed real technique and thoughtful plating, and the staff matched that quality — warm, attentive, and genuinely friendly throughout the meal, which made the whole evening feel even more special.

But it's the duck that will bring me back — it's easily some of the best I've had in the city, and worth the trip on its own.

Perfect for a special dinner or anytime you want to treat yourself to a proper duck course done right. Highly recommend requesting the duck in advance if they offer it, as it's clearly the kitchen's showcase dish.

text.text:
We had such a great experience at Happy Crane and are already looking forward to coming back!

The service really stood out—attentive, thoughtful, and genuinely warm. Our waiter noticed we were dining with our 4-year-old and kindly offered some off-the-menu modifications just for her, which we really appreciated. It made such a difference, and she ended up *devouring* the skirt steak dish and even asked for more (which basically says everything you need to know!).

Food-wise, there were some real highlights. The pidan tofu was a favorite—super flavorful and beautifully done. The lobster noodles are an absolute MUST ORDER (if they haven’t sold out!). The live lobster was fresh and lightly fried, paired with egg noodles in a rich, savory sauce that we couldn’t get enough of.

We also enjoyed the golden coins and scallop dumplings—they were tasty and well-executed. That said, at over $10 per dumpling, we did feel the portion/value didn’t quite match the price point, so we might try other dishes next time instead.

The cocktails were another pleasant surprise—creative, well-balanced, and fun to explore.

Overall, this was a fantastic dining experience with great service, memorable dishes, and a welcoming atmosphere for families. We’ll definitely be back for more!

text.text:
Finally got to try The Happy Crane, and was fortunate enough to snag a seating for their highly anticipated CNY collab with Sun Moon Studio.

Amazing menu, featuring the house specials of each restaurant. Loved their creative takes on traditional Chinese dishes. Favorite bites included their collab lobster dumpling, the crab butter lo mein, and Buddha jumps over the wall soup. So many courses with high quality ingredients that made the meal worth every penny.

Cannot wait to come back next month to try the rest of their regular Ala carte menu!

text.text:
Best Peking duck I’ve had in the Bay Area. Skin is thick and crispy, and I enjoyed the addition of sliced pluots.

As for the rest of the menu, I’ve had most of it, and some standouts are the firecracker shrimp, the golden coin, the chasiu Iberico pork jowl, and their house pulled biang biang noodles. I was also a big dessert fan, especially the mango sago sorbet with passionfruit.

Cocktails are excellent as well, East meets West flavors blend effortlessly.

originalText.text:

As for the rest of the menu, I’ve had most of it, and some standouts are the firecracker shrimp, the golden coin, the chasiu Iberico pork jowl, and their house pulled biang biang noodles. I was also a big dessert fan, especially the mango sago sorbet with passionfruit.

Cocktails are excellent as well, East meets West flavors blend effortlessly.

text.text:
We opted for à la carte and went for 3 small, 2 medium, one large dish, and dessert(s). All dishes are meant to be shared and for us, we felt they were appropriately sized and absolutely delightful. Came hungry, left full. I loved their expression of each dish and timing by staff was well executed, not oppressive, and despite the packed house I never felt the stress of a full Saturday night through staff. They are popular right now, take that as you will. My friend and I were immersed in spending time together, we enjoyed not being checked in on excessively.

The stand out for us were the golden coin, quail (loved the salt and sensation), lobster noodles, and our desserts. Would return.
